title: The Last Days of Franco
content:
Montserrat Roig’s classic novel, The Time of Cherries, captures a still point in the history of Barcelona before great change. When the novel ends, no one has any idea that within 18 months, the old dictator Francisco Franco will be gone. The Time of Cherries was originally published in Catalan in 1976, the year after Franco finally died. Half a century later, Roig’s excavation of family life in a period of historical flux is now available in English. It has become an essential book in post-Franco Catalonia, providing images of a culture casting off its past.

The novel is infused with vivid characters and a sharply detailed vision of middle-class Barcelona before democracy was restored. It revolves around Natàlia, a former student activist now approaching age 40, as she deals with unfinished family business. This includes navigating her conservative brother, fearful sister-in-law, and the stifling political atmosphere she had abruptly fled 12 years earlier. Soon after its publication, the novel became a massive hit, available in cheap paperbacks across downtown Barcelona kiosks. Readers were struck by the freshness of her tone and the fearless depiction of a new generation.

The book featured a sexual frankness that came as a profound relief in a country where art had been heavily censored. When Roig portrays the older generation, many characters are emotionally and spiritually maimed by the long years of dictatorship. However, she is careful to make the generational gap complex and ambiguous rather than simple or predictable. The middle class in Barcelona had remained largely undisturbed by the World Wars and the Spanish Civil War. Their innate conservatism persisted as they kept their heads down and focused on survival during the darkest days of the regime.
